entries.name: ctx
entries.description: Attacker took over abandoned package and uploaded versions that exfiltrate AWS credentials from environment variables
indicators: sends AWS credentials to remote endpoint

entries.name: phpass
entries.description: Companion malicious PyPI package alongside ctx hijack; exfiltrates AWS credentials

entries.name: colorsama
entries.description: Typosquat of colorama; exfiltrates Discord tokens, browser cookies, system info

entries.name: djanga
entries.description: Typosquat of django; installs information stealer

entries.name: pip-helper
entries.description: Dependency confusion attack package masquerading as internal Python utility; collects host data and posts to attacker URL

entries.name: ultralytics
entries.description: GitHub Actions cache poisoning combined with a script-injection in the release workflow pushed two malicious versions of the popular YOLO/object-detection package to PyPI; users were dropped XMRig cryptominer on install.

entries.name: torchtriton
entries.description: Dependency-confusion attack against the PyTorch nightly channel: an attacker uploaded a malicious `torchtriton` to public PyPI, which `pip` resolved in preference to PyTorch's private index. The package exfiltrated ~/.ssh, /etc/hosts, and environment vars.

entries.name: aiocpa
entries.description: Backdoor added to a previously-clean async CryptoPay client; new release shipped an obfuscated payload that stole wallet keys. Detected by Phylum.

entries.name: request
entries.description: Long-running typosquat of `requests`; one of the most-installed accidental imports on PyPI. Various authors have used the name to push installer-stage payloads.

entries.name: python-requests
entries.description: Typosquat of `requests` using the obvious `python-` prefix; multiple malicious uploads under this name historically.

entries.name: requests3
entries.description: Originally a legitimate experimental fork, the name is repeatedly registered by attackers between abandoned periods. Treat as suspicious unless installed from the canonical author.

entries.name: colourama
entries.description: British-spelling typosquat of `colorama`; published a clipboard hijacker that replaced cryptocurrency addresses in copied text.

entries.name: python-colorama
entries.description: Persistent typosquat of `colorama` using the `python-` prefix; ESET tracked dozens of uploads dropping `cstealer`-style infostealers.

entries.name: ssh-decorator
entries.description: Early PyPI malware: a Python SSH-wrapper decorator that exfiltrated SSH connection credentials (host/user/key/password) to an attacker host. Among the first publicly documented PyPI supply-chain incidents.

entries.name: diango
entries.description: Typosquat of django from the original SK-CSIRT 10-package PyPI malware discovery. PyPI mirrored package payloads to attacker host at install time.

entries.name: djago
entries.description: Another Django typosquat from the SK-CSIRT 2017 disclosure. Same payload as diango.

entries.name: urllib
entries.description: Typosquat of urllib3 from the SK-CSIRT 2017 wave. PyPI removed the package shortly after disclosure.

entries.name: setup-tools
entries.description: Typosquat of setuptools from the SK-CSIRT 2017 wave. Same payload pattern as ssh-decorator.

entries.name: python3-dateutil
entries.description: Typosquat of python-dateutil that exfiltrated arbitrary files and SSH keys to an attacker server. Published alongside the jeIlyfish typosquat.

entries.name: jeIlyfish
entries.description: Typosquat of jellyfish using a capital I in place of a lowercase l. Stole SSH keys and env vars; the second-stage payload was retrieved from a remote host on first import.

entries.name: cupy
entries.description: Homoglyph / case-variant typosquat of the legitimate CuPy CUDA Python package. Exfiltrated environment variables on import; targeted ML developers.

entries.name: importlib_metadata
entries.description: Underscore variant of `importlib-metadata`. Member of a 2021 typosquat campaign that targeted high-traffic Python stdlib-adjacent packages.

entries.name: httpslib
entries.description: Part of the W4SP Stealer campaign. Hundreds of related PyPI packages bundled a Python info-stealer that collected Discord tokens, browser cookies, and saved passwords, exfiltrating to anonymous file hosts.

entries.name: libhttps
entries.description: Member of the W4SP Stealer family alongside httpslib and httpserver-python; identical info-stealer payload.

entries.name: langchain-core-utils
entries.description: Typosquat targeting the LangChain ecosystem to capitalize on the LLM-tooling boom. Carried an info-stealer that exfiltrated OpenAI API keys and other developer secrets from environment variables.

entries.name: chatgpt-python
entries.description: Typosquat of `openai` and unofficial chatgpt clients. Exfiltrated environment variables and a list of installed Python packages to an attacker server.

entries.name: pycord-self
entries.description: Backdoored Discord self-bot library. The 2.7.0 release added an obfuscated payload that posted Discord tokens, billing info, and machine fingerprints to an attacker webhook on import.

entries.name: huggingface-cli
entries.description: Squatted on a name LLMs frequently hallucinate when asked for the Hugging Face CLI install command (the real one is `huggingface_hub`). Once-popular typosquat name attackers can use as a slop-squat landing page.

entries.name: aiohttp-socks3
entries.description: Slop-squat: published under a name LLMs commonly hallucinate when asked for a SOCKS-capable aiohttp transport. Followed the Lasso 'AI package hallucinations' research that documented the slop-squat attack class.

entries.name: coloredtxt
entries.description: Typosquat of colorama / colorlog. Exfiltrated saved browser passwords, Discord tokens, and crypto wallet keys via a Python-side info-stealer on every import.

entries.name: eth-security-auditor
entries.description: Part of the 'TrapDoor' supply-chain campaign (Socket, from 2026-05-22) — the first to weaponize npm, PyPI and crates.io simultaneously, disguising a crypto-focused credential stealer as developer/security tools aimed at crypto/DeFi/Solana/AI developers. Steals SSH keys, Sui/Solana/Aptos wallet keystores, AWS credentials, GitHub tokens, browser profile + wallet-extension data, env vars and API keys. PyPI execution path: import-time fetch+exec of remote JavaScript.
indicators: part of the multi-ecosystem 'TrapDoor' campaign (npm+PyPI+crates, from 2026-05-22)
disguised as a crypto/DeFi/web3 security or dev tool
exfiltrates SSH keys, crypto wallet keystores, AWS/GitHub creds, browser data

entries.name: solana-cli-py
entries.description: Part of the JFrog-disclosed 'Solana FakeFix' campaign (2026-06-11): ~25 npm/PyPI packages impersonating Solana developer tooling (@solana/web3.js, @solana/spl-token) and luring developers with fake 'stable/fixed/patched/LTS' builds. On install/import it steals Solana keypairs (~/.config/solana/id.json), SSH keys (~/.ssh/id_rsa), AWS credentials (~/.aws/credentials), source-control tokens, and .env secrets (vars named KEY/SECRET/MNEMONIC/TOKEN).

entries.name: solana-web3
entries.description: Part of the JFrog-disclosed 'Solana FakeFix' campaign (2026-06-11): ~25 npm/PyPI packages impersonating Solana developer tooling (@solana/web3.js, @solana/spl-token) and luring developers with fake 'stable/fixed/patched/LTS' builds. On install/import it steals Solana keypairs (~/.config/solana/id.json), SSH keys (~/.ssh/id_rsa), AWS credentials (~/.aws/credentials), source-control tokens, and .env secrets (vars named KEY/SECRET/MNEMONIC/TOKEN).

entries.name: solana-web3-py

entries.name: guardrails-ai
entries.description: Compromised in the 'Mini Shai-Hulud' self-propagating worm (TeamPCP, disclosed 2026-05-12; CVE-2026-45321) that hit 160+ npm/PyPI packages incl. TanStack, UiPath and Mistral AI. The worm steals developer and cloud credentials, self-propagates through the ecosystem, and can drop a persistent destructive daemon that wipes developer home directories. Only this version is malicious — upgrade to a clean release published after 2026-05-12.
indicators: only version 0.10.1 is malicious (Mini Shai-Hulud / TeamPCP)
self-propagating worm; steals dev + cloud credentials
may drop a persistent daemon that wipes home directories

entries.name: mistralai
entries.description: Compromised in the 'Mini Shai-Hulud' self-propagating worm (TeamPCP, disclosed 2026-05-12; CVE-2026-45321) that hit 160+ npm/PyPI packages incl. TanStack, UiPath and Mistral AI. The worm steals developer and cloud credentials, self-propagates through the ecosystem, and can drop a persistent destructive daemon that wipes developer home directories. Only this version is malicious — upgrade to a clean release published after 2026-05-12.
indicators: only version 2.4.6 is malicious (Mini Shai-Hulud / TeamPCP)
self-propagating worm; steals dev + cloud credentials
may drop a persistent daemon that wipes home directories

entries.name: bramin
entries.description: Compromised in the 'Miasma' worm's Hades wave (2026-06-07): 19 PyPI packages (37 malicious wheels) that install a Python .pth startup hook so a Bun-powered JavaScript credential stealer runs on EVERY Python interpreter startup (persisting beyond the package itself). Part of the broader Miasma campaign Socket tracked across 448 npm+PyPI artifacts. Only the listed versions are malicious.
indicators: installs a Python .pth startup hook (runs on every interpreter start)
only the listed versions are malicious
